disciplines.structures: Load-bearing geometry, frames, spars, skins, joints, stiffness
disciplines.mechanisms: Linkages, hinges, gears, cams, deployment, tilt/rotation devices
disciplines.actuation: Motors, servos, brakes, reducers and their mounting/replacement
disciplines.power: Battery, solar generation, charging, distribution, connectors
disciplines.electronics: Boards, drivers, wiring, signal routing, grounding
disciplines.gnc: Attitude/position sensing and control hardware evidence (mock level)
disciplines.payload: Mission equipment bay, sensor mounts, removable inserts
disciplines.integration: Datums, interfaces, harness routing, service access, assembly order

required_subsystems.function: Tilt axis with bearing seats, servo linkage, rotation clearance arc
Motor pod with replaceable motor module and access cover
Wing spar carrying nacelle loads into fuselage spine; V-tail/ruddervator and landing gear attach structure
Slide-in li-ion battery pack bay with CG adjustment, BMS and connector access
Flight controller/autopilot tray, telemetry and VTX modules, motor power vs signal wire separation across tilt joint
IMU, pitot/airspeed sensor, GPS mount evidence near CG (mock level)
Nose payload/sensor bay with removable insert and service window
Nacelle interface datums, harness routing through tilt axis, hatches, connection types and load-path closure

subsystem_taxonomy.completeness_rule: Every required_subsystem must appear in part_tree with at least one evidence_feature in geometry_ops; a missing subsystem is a scorecard failure, not a style issue.

criteria.good: Parts are integrated only when service access, inspection, and interface clarity remain visible.
criteria.bad: Monolithic body hides removable modules, datums, or inspection paths.
criteria.loop_feedback: Consolidate only low-risk covers or labels; keep service modules, datums, and inspection windows visible.

criteria.good: Fastener patterns, pin joints, slots, bosses, and mating clearances are explicitly represented.
criteria.bad: Joints are described in prose but not represented as holes, bosses, pins, slots, or datums.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add visible fastener holes, bosses, joint pins, and clearance notes for each critical interface.

criteria.good: A reviewer can see what to inspect and why without opening raw JSON.
criteria.bad: Design has no callouts, access windows, labels, datum pads, or verification focus.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add inspection callouts, datum labels, visible windows, and verification checks tied to geometry features.

criteria.good: Critical joints state locking method (locknut, insert, washer, adhesive note) and torque/preload intent; bolt patterns match load direction.
criteria.bad: Fasteners appear as bare holes with no locking, washer, engagement-length, or preload language anywhere.
criteria.loop_feedback: For each critical joint, name the locking feature, add washer/insert geometry, and state a torque or preload intent note.

criteria.good: Threaded connections into printed or soft material use inserts or bosses with adequate engagement depth (~1.5x diameter) and wall around the hole.
criteria.bad: Screws thread directly into thin printed walls or blind features with no boss, insert, or depth note.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add insert bosses with engagement depth >= 1.5x screw diameter and surrounding wall thickness for every threaded joint.

criteria.good: P0 internal_feature_targets appear as part_tree children and geometry_ops ids or targets.
criteria.bad: P0 lists features but geometry_ops are generic boxes at the origin.
criteria.loop_feedback: Map every P0 feature target into a named part_tree child and at least one geometry_op id or target.

criteria.good: Most geometry_ops include args.at coordinates distributed across the local subsystem volume.
criteria.bad: Geometry is stacked at [0,0,0] or lacks coordinates.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add coordinate-bearing geometry_ops across upstream/downstream/side/service regions instead of stacking features.

criteria.good: Uses drill, pocket, boss, channel, cylinder, chamfer, fillet, pattern, and engrave where appropriate.
criteria.bad: Primary output is a rectangular box with shallow labels.
criteria.loop_feedback: Replace box-only geometry with interface bosses, service pockets, drilled holes, channels, ribs, and labels.

criteria.good: Motion claims are grounded in a named mechanism type (four-bar linkage, cam, rack-and-pinion, lead screw, ratchet, geneva) with its pivot/axis geometry present.
criteria.bad: Design claims motion ('arm rotates', 'finger flexes') but no pivot holes, gear teeth dummies, cam profiles, or linkage bars exist in geometry.
criteria.loop_feedback: Name the mechanism type behind every motion claim and add its pivot holes, links, or cam/gear dummy geometry.

criteria.good: Battery, sensor, actuator, cartridge, payload, cable, or joint modules remain removable without full teardown.
criteria.bad: Functional modules are fused into a closed body.
criteria.loop_feedback: Separate the critical service module and add a visible removal path, latch, pin, or slide rail.

criteria.good: Harnesses, tubes, or service channels are routed through visible raceways or protected channels.
criteria.bad: Cables are buried inside closed links or shells with no inspection feature.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add external or inspectable harness channels with strain relief and access covers.

criteria.good: Access is represented by geometry: hatch, pocket, channel, fastener pattern, slot, window, or removable cover.
criteria.bad: Access is claimed only in prose.
criteria.loop_feedback: Convert service-access text into physical geometry_ops: cover, pocket, slot, window, and fastener pattern.

criteria.good: Every cable exit shows strain relief geometry (clamp, grommet, tie point) and routing respects a stated minimum bend radius away from moving joints and sharp edges.
criteria.bad: Cables exit bare holes, cross hinge lines unsupported, or bend around sharp printed edges with no relief feature.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add strain-relief clamps or grommets at every cable exit, state a minimum bend radius, and reroute harnesses away from hinge lines and sharp edges.

criteria.good: Connectors are reachable without disassembling structure, have finger/tool clearance, and mention keying or labeling to prevent mis-mating.
criteria.bad: Connectors face closed walls, sit in unreachable pockets, or identical adjacent connectors have no keying or label.
criteria.loop_feedback: Reorient connectors toward access openings, add finger clearance pockets, and engrave keying/ID labels next to each connector.

criteria.good: Primary locating faces, rail guides, axis rings, spar sockets, or mating pads are visible and labeled.
criteria.bad: Interfaces exist only as abstract neighbor references.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add visible datum pads or axis/rail/socket features and label their mating function.

criteria.good: Critical slip, snap, pin, hinge, and slide interfaces state plausible clearances.
criteria.bad: Moving/removable joints have no clearance or fit language.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add clearance_mm or tolerance notes for hinge pins, slide rails, sockets, and removable cartridges.

criteria.good: Each subsystem has at least three adjacent interfaces or datums.
criteria.bad: Subsystem is isolated and does not show assembly neighbors.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add at least three adjacent interfaces: mount, service, electrical/data, flow, or inspection datum.

criteria.good: Fits are named with standard intent: clearance fit for sliding, transition for locating, interference/press only where justified, with a numeric allowance.
criteria.bad: All mating diameters are modeled line-to-line with identical nominal dimensions and no fit class or allowance.
criteria.loop_feedback: Classify each mating diameter as clearance/transition/press fit and add a numeric allowance instead of line-to-line nominals.

criteria.good: Holes, slots, and pockets match real catalog hardware sizes (M3/M4 clearance holes, standard bearing OD, common pin diameters).
criteria.bad: Hardware features use arbitrary dimensions that match no purchasable fastener, bearing, or pin.
criteria.loop_feedback: Snap hole, pin, and bearing dimensions to standard catalog sizes and name the intended hardware in callouts.

criteria.title: First-Order Structural Sizing Judgment
criteria.purpose: Dimensions must be defended by a load path and a first-order margin, not chosen by appearance. Taught here, measured by analysis_estimate.sizing (computed bending stress / Factor of Safety vs material allowable). Educational first-order check, NOT a certified structural analysis.

criteria.good: Each primary structural member states what load it carries and how it reacts into the datum (cantilever root, simply supported, axial, shear pin).
criteria.bad: Members exist with no stated load, support condition, or reaction path.
criteria.loop_feedback: Declare each primary member's load case (force/moment), support condition, and reaction into the datum so its cross-section can be sized.

criteria.good: Cross-sections give a stated or derivable Factor of Safety >= 1.5 against the declared load using the part material allowable.
criteria.bad: No margin stated; cross-sections appear arbitrary relative to load.
criteria.loop_feedback: Size primary members to a Factor of Safety >= 1.5: increase section depth, add ribs/bracing, or raise wall thickness where the computed bending stress approaches the material allowable.

criteria.good: Long members use depth/section modulus (ribs, I/box section, tube) appropriate to bending, not flat slender bars.
criteria.bad: Long members are thin slender bars with low section modulus that buckle or deflect.
criteria.loop_feedback: Raise section modulus on long members: add depth, ribs, a box/tube section, or bracing instead of a flat slender bar.

criteria.good: Material and its allowable (yield/tensile, with FDM layer-adhesion knockdown for printed parts) are named and used to justify wall and section.
criteria.bad: Material is unnamed or allowable stress is never referenced when sizing.
criteria.loop_feedback: Name the material allowable (and FDM knockdown for printed parts) and use it when justifying wall thickness and section size.

criteria.good: Bolted/pinned joints state fastener size, count, and a shear/preload rationale matched to the joint load.
criteria.bad: Joints show holes with no fastener size, count, or shear/preload justification.
criteria.loop_feedback: For each loaded joint state fastener size, count, and shear/preload capacity vs the joint load.

criteria.good: Material/process, wall assumptions, build direction, and supports are explicit enough for design review.
criteria.bad: Manufacturing process is absent or contradicts geometry.
criteria.loop_feedback: State material/process, min wall, build direction, and support implications in cad_brief and print_profile.

criteria.good: Verify/risk items reference real features or interfaces from part_tree/geometry_ops.
criteria.bad: Verification is generic pass/warn text not tied to features.
criteria.loop_feedback: Tie each verification check to a named geometry feature, datum, interface, or service access path.

criteria.good: Risks cover blocked access, weak interfaces, tolerance mismatch, process limitations, and unsafe overclaiming.
criteria.bad: Risks are missing or only mention vague quality concerns.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add concrete risks for blocked service, datum loss, clearance conflict, unsupported geometry, and unsafe overclaiming.

criteria.good: Each stated requirement maps to a verification method (inspection, demonstration, analysis, test) tied to a named feature or interface.
criteria.bad: Requirements and verification lists exist independently with no traceable pairing.
criteria.loop_feedback: Pair every requirement with one verification method and the geometry feature or interface that provides the evidence.

criteria.good: Cross-subsystem interfaces state owner, mating geometry, and what is exchanged (load, power, data, fluid), like a miniature interface control entry.
criteria.bad: Subsystems reference each other by name only, with no statement of what crosses the interface.
criteria.loop_feedback: For each interface, state both sides, the mating feature, and the exchanged quantity (load/power/data/fluid).

criteria.good: Tilt axis, bearing seats, servo/linkage access, and nacelle rotation clearance are visible.
criteria.bad: Nacelle is a generic motor pod without tilt-axis evidence.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add tilt-axis bearing seats, servo linkage cover, rotation clearance arc, and nacelle interface datums.

criteria.good: Wing spar socket, fuselage spine, and payload/battery service paths are separate and inspectable.
criteria.bad: Wing/fuselage is a blended block with no interface features.
criteria.loop_feedback: Expose wing spar sockets, fuselage spine datum, payload bay, and slide-in battery service feature.

criteria.good: Output remains educational mockup, avoiding operational flight tuning or control-law details.
criteria.bad: Design claims BVLOS capability, flight readiness, or actionable aircraft tuning.
criteria.loop_feedback: Frame VTOL features as educational mock assembly evidence, not flight-qualified or operational design.

criteria.good: Tilt mechanism loads route through bearing seats into wing structure via named fittings, and motor/servo modules remain replaceable through access covers.
criteria.bad: Nacelle attaches by an undefined blend; motor and servo are entombed with no removal path or load fitting.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add named tilt-bearing fittings into the wing spar path and removable motor/servo covers on each nacelle.

criteria.title: Electronics, Signal, and Motor Wiring Judgment

criteria.good: Motor power lines and sensor/encoder signal lines run in separate channels or are spaced/crossed at right angles, with the separation visible in routing geometry.
criteria.bad: Power and signal wires share one tight bundle along the whole route with no separation or shielding note.
criteria.loop_feedback: Split power and signal into separate raceways or add spacing/shielding notes where they must share a channel.

criteria.good: Each electronics module states its grounding scheme (star point, chassis bond, isolated) and signal returns route alongside their signals.
criteria.bad: Grounding is unmentioned; returns are implied through structure with no bond feature or note.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add a named ground/bond feature per module and state how each signal's return path is routed.

criteria.good: PCBs mount on standoffs with keep-out clearance to walls and moving parts, connector edges face access openings, and mounting holes match standard board patterns.
criteria.bad: Boards are floated or glued in cavities with no standoffs, clearance, or connector orientation logic.
criteria.loop_feedback: Mount each board on standoffs with stated keep-out clearance and orient connector edges toward access openings.

criteria.good: Every inter-part interface names a specific connection type (bolted, threaded_insert, snap_fit, press_fit, dovetail_slide, clevis_pin, revolute_bearing, band_clamp, adhesive_lap, o_ring, connector_mate, harness_passthrough) with its DOF and a numeric clearance.
criteria.bad: Interfaces are labeled only 'mount' or 'attach' with no connection type, DOF, or clearance.
criteria.loop_feedback: For each interface pick a connection type from the taxonomy, state its DOF (0/1R/1T/1R1T/3R), and add a numeric clearance and the matching geometry feature.

criteria.good: Parts can be installed in a stated order where every fastener, connector, and mating feature stays reachable; no installed part blocks access to a later one; last-installed and captive parts are identified.
criteria.bad: Assembly order is unstated or impossible — a part traps another, or a fastener ends up behind a wall with no access.
criteria.loop_feedback: State an installation order and verify each joint is reachable when it is made; reorder or add access openings where a part would trap another.

criteria.good: One primary datum part anchors the assembly; each other part locates off a named parent datum using a 3-2-1 scheme, so tolerance does not accumulate uncontrolled across the stack.
criteria.bad: Parts float with no shared datum, or each part references a different feature so stack-up grows part-to-part.
criteria.loop_feedback: Name one primary datum part, route each part's location back to it through one parent datum, and keep critical fits off the primary datum to limit stack-up.

criteria.good: Every part's primary load traces through named interfaces to the base/ground; the load path closes for all parts and high-load joints use load-appropriate connection types.
criteria.bad: One or more parts carry load with no continuous path to ground, or a high-load joint uses a low-load connection (e.g. snap-fit holding a structural member).
criteria.loop_feedback: Trace each part's main load to the base through named interfaces; upgrade any load-bearing joint from snap/adhesive to bolted/pinned/clamped and add the fitting where the path breaks.

criteria.good: Compared to the previous version the design moves the right direction: fewer interfaces, parts consolidated where service is preserved, open load paths closed, and redundancy added only at critical joints.
criteria.bad: Iteration adds interfaces or loose parts without justification, or consolidates in a way that buries service access.
criteria.loop_feedback: Drive the next version toward fewer justified interfaces, consolidated printable parts that keep service access, and closed load paths; add redundancy only at safety-critical joints.

criteria.good: The assembly uses the fewest interfaces that still allow service and manufacture; each remaining interface is justified by service, motion, manufacture split, or load transfer.
criteria.bad: Many redundant or unexplained interfaces, or arbitrary part splits that add joints without a service/manufacture reason.
criteria.loop_feedback: Merge interfaces that exist for no service/motion/manufacture/load reason; keep a split only when it earns its joint.

citable_sources.title: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook SP-2016-6105 Rev2
1800 Mechanical Movements, Devices and Appliances (Hiscox, 1899, public domain)

local_reading_only.title: Marks' Standard Handbook for Mechanical Engineers
Bruhn - Analysis and Design of Flight Vehicle Structures
Shapiro - Dynamics and Thermodynamics of Compressible Fluid Flow
target_topics: flow vocabulary for duct/inlet geometry grounding

local_reading_only.title: The Art and Science of Analog Circuit Design (PCB)
Leishman - Principles of Helicopter Aerodynamics (frontmatter sample only, 42pp)
